extraction.primary_complaint: Right shoulder pain and weakness following a work-related lifting injury
functional_limitations: Unable to lift objects above shoulder height
Cannot perform overhead reaching for daily tasks
Disturbed sleep due to shoulder pain
Unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation
objective_measurements: Active forward flexion: 95 degrees (limited by pain)
Strength testing: 3/5 supraspinatus on empty can test

conservative_treatments_attempted.outcome: minimal improvement, pain persisted with overhead activity
2 weeks of partial relief, then return to baseline symptoms
inadequate pain control, GI intolerance developed

imaging_findings.key_findings: X-ray (01/22/2025): AC joint arthrosis Grade II. No calcific deposits. No significant glenohumeral arthrosis.; MRI (01/22/2025): Full-thickness supraspinatus tear measuring 2.1 cm with retraction to the musculotendinous junction. Moderate subacromial bursitis. Intact subscapularis.

extraction.requested_procedure: Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, right shoulder - CPT 29827
extraction.surgical_approach_if_mentioned: Arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair

denial_risk_flags.label: Physical Therapy Duration Below Typical Threshold
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Physical therapy was documented for only 8 weeks. Many BCBS policies for CPT 29827 require a minimum of 6–12 weeks of formal PT, but reviewers may scrutinize whether 8 weeks is sufficient given the severity of the tear and the short relief duration.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Add documentation clarifying the number of PT sessions attended, the specific exercises performed, and objective functional measurements at discharge to demonstrate that PT was maximally attempted and failed.

denial_risk_flags.label: Single Corticosteroid Injection — No Follow-Up Attempt
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Only one corticosteroid injection is documented, providing only 2 weeks of partial relief. Some payers expect documentation of a repeat injection or alternative injection therapy before approving surgical intervention.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Document clinical rationale for not repeating the injection (e.g., full-thickness tear size, rapid symptom recurrence, or contraindication) to preempt a denial based on insufficient injection trials.

denial_risk_flags.label: Work-Related Injury — Workers' Comp Coordination
denial_risk_flags.explanation: The chart documents a work-related lifting injury as the mechanism of injury. BCBS may require confirmation that this is not a workers' compensation claim, or may coordinate benefits, potentially delaying or denying authorization.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Clarify in the chart whether this claim is being submitted to BCBS as primary payer or whether a workers' compensation carrier is involved, and document accordingly.

denial_risk_flags.label: Attending Surgeon Name Is Placeholder
denial_risk_flags.explanation: The attending surgeon is listed as Dr. Michael Torres in the chart, which is a placeholder token. The requesting provider field submitted to BCBS lists Dr. Michael Torres, creating a potential discrepancy that may trigger a denial or request for additional information.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Confirm that Dr. Michael Torres resolves to Dr. Michael Torres and ensure the attending surgeon name is consistently documented across all submitted authorization materials.

denial_risk_flags.label: Conservative Care Dates Incomplete — No Start/End Clarity
denial_risk_flags.explanation: Physical therapy dates are listed as a single placeholder September-October 2024 without explicit start and end dates, making it difficult for reviewers to confirm the 8-week duration claim independently.
denial_risk_flags.recommendation: Provide explicit start and end dates for the physical therapy course (e.g., September-October 2024 through [end date]) to allow payer reviewers to verify the stated 8-week duration.

diagnosis_codes.note: M75.121 (full-thickness rotator cuff tear, right shoulder) is a clinically appropriate and direct medical necessity indication for CPT 29827 arthroscopic rotator cuff repair.

conservative_treatment_duration.note: 2 of 2 duration-eligible treatments have an explicit numeric duration (100%). Meets the 50% threshold.

surgical_approach.note: The surgical approach is explicitly documented as 'Arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair,' which is a specific, procedure-appropriate technique consistent with CPT 29827.

cpt_code_valid.note: CPT 29827 is a recognized orthopedic surgical code.

letter:
July 5, 2026
Blue Cross Blue Shield
Prior Authorization Department

Re: Kim, Rachel | DOB: 04/15/1975 | Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair (CPT 29827) | M75.121, M75.111
Member ID: See attached insurance card
Patient: Kim, Rachel
Date of Birth: 04/15/1975
Procedure: Arthroscopic Rotator Cuff Repair
CPT Code: 29827

Dear Prior Authorization Reviewer,

CLINICAL HISTORY AND PRESENTING COMPLAINT

This patient presents with an eight-month history of right shoulder pain and weakness following a work-related lifting injury, reporting pain rated 7/10 at rest and 9/10 with overhead activity. Range of motion assessment demonstrates active forward flexion limited to 95 degrees, active abduction to 80 degrees, internal rotation to 40 degrees, and external rotation to 55 degrees, with strength testing revealing 3/5 supraspinatus function on empty can test and an American Shoulder and Elbow Surgeons (ASES) score of 35.2 out of 100. The patient is unable to lift objects above shoulder height, cannot perform overhead reaching for daily tasks, experiences disturbed sleep due to shoulder pain, and is unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation.

DIAGNOSIS

The patient carries confirmed diagnoses of complete rotator cuff tear of the right shoulder (M75.121) and incomplete rotator cuff tear of the right shoulder (M75.111), substantiated by advanced imaging as detailed below.

FUNCTIONAL LIMITATIONS

1. Unable to lift objects above shoulder height
2. Cannot perform overhead reaching for daily tasks
3. Disturbed sleep due to shoulder pain
4. Unable to return to work duties requiring arm elevation

CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT HISTORY

1. Physical Therapy (8 weeks): The patient underwent a course of physical therapy resulting in minimal improvement, with pain persisting with overhead activity. Functional gains were insufficient to restore meaningful mobility or occupational capacity.

2. Corticosteroid Injection — Kenalog 40mg: A corticosteroid injection was administered, providing two weeks of partial relief before symptoms returned to baseline. Sustained benefit was not achieved.

3. NSAIDs — Meloxicam 15mg daily (3 months): The patient was maintained on meloxicam 15mg daily, which provided inadequate pain control, and GI intolerance subsequently developed, necessitating discontinuation.

REQUESTED PROCEDURE

Arthroscopic rotator cuff repair, right shoulder (CPT 29827), utilizing an arthroscopic with mini-open rotator cuff repair approach.

The patient's BMI of 27.4 and ASA II classification have been factored into the perioperative surgical plan.

Advanced imaging confirms the structural basis for the requested intervention. X-ray obtained on 01/22/2025 demonstrates Grade II AC joint arthrosis without calcific deposits and without significant glenohumeral arthrosis. MRI obtained on 01/22/2025 reveals a full-thickness supraspinatus tear measuring 2.1 cm with retraction to the musculotendinous junction, accompanied by moderate subacromial bursitis, with an intact subscapularis tendon. These findings are consistent with the patient's clinical presentation and confirm that the degree of structural compromise is not amenable to further conservative management.

MEDICAL NECESSITY SUMMARY

This patient has sustained a structurally confirmed, full-thickness supraspinatus tear with measurable retraction, resulting in profound functional compromise across all domains of shoulder-dependent activity. Three distinct conservative treatment modalities — physical therapy, corticosteroid injection, and NSAID pharmacotherapy — have been pursued without achieving durable relief or functional restoration. The patient's ASES score of 35.2/100 and objective range of motion deficits underscore the severity of impairment. Surgical intervention via arthroscopic rotator cuff repair is medically necessary to restore structural integrity, prevent further tendon retraction and muscle atrophy, and enable the patient to resume activities of daily living. Continued delay risks irreversible progression of the tear and permanent loss of shoulder function. Authorization for CPT 29827 is respectfully requested.

Sincerely,
Michael Torres, MD
Summit Orthopedic Group
